title: Read-error path returns archivedTo='' but no archive was written
description: When fsPromises.readFile throws a non-ENOENT error (EACCES, ENOTDIR, EISDIR, EIO, etc.), loadState returns { kind: 'corrupt', archivedTo: '', message }. The empty-string archivedTo is structurally indistinguishable from a successful archive at the type level (type still requires `archivedTo: string`), and tools/start.ts forwards it verbatim into the surfaced state-corrupt warning at lines 60-64 — so the user receives a warning whose archivedTo field is the empty string, implying an archive exists when none was created. The contract A6 calls this a 'wrapped read-error message' but does not authorize a phantom archive path. Cycle 1's C002/C010 lesson was about distinguishing error codes; this regresses on the new surface by producing a misleading payload for the very codes it is supposed to distinguish.
impact: Users (and cycle 4's recovery flow) may attempt to read the archive path '' to diagnose the corruption, hitting a confusing ENOENT on the empty-string path. The corrupt branch's guarantee in A4 ('archivedTo is an absolute path that exists on disk') is violated for the EACCES sub-case, breaking the invariant the consolidator can rely on to drive recovery UX.
recommendation: Either (a) widen LoadStateResult so the corrupt variant has `archivedTo?: string` and only set it when an archive really was written (preferred — keeps the field truthful), or (b) introduce a fourth kind `read-error` distinct from corrupt for unreadable-but-present files. Update tools/start.ts to omit archivedTo from the warning when no archive exists. T-058 only checks the message text, so the bug is invisible to the current test suite.

title: Archive write failure rejects loadState, swallowing the corruption signal
description: When JSON.parse fails, loadState calls `await archiveCorruptFile(...)` (line 49) before constructing the corrupt result. If the archive write itself fails — disk full (ENOSPC), permissions (EACCES on the directory), or the .sui-deepbook-course/ entry is now a file (ENOTDIR) — archiveCorruptFile rejects, and that rejection propagates unchanged out of loadState. The caller (tools/start.ts) has no try/catch around `await loadState(projectRoot)`, so the entire start tool rejects, the SDK turns the rejection into an MCP tool error, and the user sees a generic transport failure instead of a structured 'state-corrupt' warning. The original corruption fact is lost — the archive failure mode masquerades as an unrelated bug. Same pattern repeats at lines 64 (missing schema_version branch) and 85 (schema validation branch).
impact: A second-order failure (disk full / permission flip on the archive directory) erases the primary corruption diagnostic. The student cannot tell whether their state.json is broken or whether the tool itself crashed; recovery flow in cycle 4 cannot key off the missing 'state-corrupt' kind. This is exactly the silent-failure class cycle 1 carry-forward C002/C010 was meant to prevent on the new surface.
recommendation: Wrap each `await archiveCorruptFile(...)` in try/catch and degrade gracefully — return { kind: 'corrupt', archivedTo: undefined, message: `corrupt; archive failed: ${err.message}` } so the corruption classification still surfaces even when archival fails. Alternatively, hoist a single helper that returns both an archivedTo and an archive-error message. Either way, never let archive I/O failure swallow the primary diagnostic.

title: saveState fsync opens a separate read-only fd, not the writeFile fd — durability is not guaranteed
description: saveState calls `await fsPromises.writeFile(tmpPath, bytes, 'utf8')` (line 132), which opens, writes, and closes its own fd internally. Then on line 135 the code opens a fresh `fs.openSync(tmpPath, 'r')` (read-only) and runs fsyncSync on that descriptor. POSIX fsync syncs the file data associated with the inode, so on Linux this typically works — but the kernel guarantees fsync flushes data only when invoked on a fd that has write access on certain filesystems (and on Windows / some network filesystems the read-only fsync is a no-op). The contract A7 says 'fsync(fd) (or fs.fsyncSync on the open tmp file)' which the implementer reads as 'any fd referencing the tmp file', but the only durably-guaranteed pattern is to open with write flags, write, fsync, close — using a single descriptor. Tests T-061 only check call ORDER (writeFile→fsync→rename), not that the fsync actually flushed pending dirty pages from writeFile's internal fd.
impact: On non-Linux filesystems or under rare crash windows, the rename completes before the tmp file's bytes are actually durable. After a power loss the canonical state.json may exist as a zero-length or torn file — the very atomicity the contract sells. The vitest suite cannot detect this; it shows up only in real crash scenarios.
recommendation: Use `fsPromises.open(tmpPath, 'w')` to get a FileHandle, call `handle.writeFile(bytes)` then `handle.sync()` then `handle.close()` — single fd, write-flagged, durably synced. Or use the lower-level fs.openSync(tmpPath, 'w'), fs.writeSync, fs.fsyncSync, fs.closeSync sequence. Either approach makes the durability contract real instead of best-effort.

title: saveState leaks tmp files when fsync, openSync, or rename fails
description: saveState writes to `state.tmp-<ts>-<rand>.json` (line 129). If any subsequent step rejects — `openSync` fails (EMFILE/ENFILE), `fsyncSync` throws (EIO), or `fsPromises.rename` rejects (the case T-062/T-063 explicitly simulate) — the tmp file is left on disk and saveState rethrows without cleanup. Each failed save accrues an orphan tmp file under .sui-deepbook-course/, which loadState ignores but which will eventually be visible to the student (and to any directory listing in cycle 4's recovery flow). T-062 explicitly proves the canonical file is intact after rename failure but does not assert the tmp is cleaned up — the test passes today even though the tmp is leaking.
impact: Repeated save failures (e.g. on a read-only filesystem or under permission flip) silently litter the state directory. A student running the tool in CI with a stale-state pre-step could see dozens of state.tmp-* files, no error path explains them, and the tmp filenames embed wall-clock timestamps + Math.random() so even cleanup-by-pattern is awkward. Borderline silent failure: the operation rejects, but the side effect persists with no signal.
recommendation: Wrap the post-writeFile portion of saveState in try/catch; on catch, attempt `fsPromises.unlink(tmpPath).catch(() => {})` before rethrowing. Document the cleanup in a comment so the next reviewer doesn't 'simplify' it away. Add a unit test that asserts `listAllTmpFiles(stateDir).length === 0` after a simulated rename failure.

title: schema_version accepts NaN, Infinity, and floats; mismatch path leaks them into user-facing message
description: The schema_version gate uses `typeof === 'number'` which accepts NaN, Infinity, -Infinity, and floats (e.g. 1.5). A state.json with `schema_version: NaN` parses successfully, passes the typeof check, then `foundVersion !== STATE_SCHEMA_VERSION` evaluates true (NaN compares unequal to everything), and the schema-mismatch message becomes 'State file has incompatible schema_version NaN. Manual migration required.' — JSON.stringify(NaN) is 'null' so the wire payload would surface a null version while the message text says 'NaN'. Not a crash, but a confusing diagnostic and a silent acceptance of nonsensical input.
impact: A user-facing warning string with 'NaN' / 'Infinity' / '1.5' values is confusing and leaks the implementer's lax type check. More importantly, the schema-mismatch payload's foundVersion field claims to be a number (per the union `{ kind: 'schema-mismatch'; foundVersion: number; ... }`) but in practice carries a non-finite number — downstream JSON serialization quietly converts it to null, and any cycle-4 logic that compares foundVersion against an integer will silently misbehave.
recommendation: Replace the schema_version gate with `Number.isInteger(parsed.schema_version) && parsed.schema_version >= 1`. Treat non-integer schema_version as corrupt-with-archive (it is malformed input, not a future schema). This also tightens the reasonable-future-schema invariant: only positive integers are valid version identifiers.

title: archiveCounter is module-level — collision possible across process restarts
description: The monotonic archive counter resides in module scope (`let archiveCounter = 0;`). It increments only within a single process lifetime. If the host process restarts (e.g. the MCP server is bounced between two student runs in the same wall-clock millisecond) and a second corruption occurs at the exact same Date.now() millisecond, the counter resets to 1 — the same suffix the previous process had after its first corruption. The archive filename is then identical, and `fsPromises.writeFile(archivePath, content)` clobbers the previous archive byte-for-byte. T-050 only proves uniqueness within a single process, not across restarts.
impact: Extremely low-probability collision (requires two distinct corruptions within the same wall-clock ms across a restart), but the failure mode is silent data loss of the earlier archive. The contract's two-distinct-archive promise is technically a single-process invariant, so this is a doc-vs-code gap, not a contract violation. Flagging because the consolidator may want to widen the test or note the limitation.
recommendation: Either include a process-startup random suffix in the counter seed (`let archiveCounter = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000);`), or append `process.pid` to the archive filename, or use crypto.randomUUID() in the archive name suffix. Any of these eliminates the cross-restart collision class.
evidence:
// Monotonic counter to ensure uniqueness within the same millisecond
let archiveCounter = 0;

title: loadState archives JSON-parseable non-objects (e.g. bare 'null', '42', '"foo"') as corrupt without distinguishing them
description: If state.json contains JSON-parseable bytes whose top level is null, a number, a string, or an array, loadState archives the file as corrupt with the message 'missing schema_version'. The contract distinguishes 'JSON-parse failure' (corrupt-with-archive) from 'unknown schema_version' (schema-mismatch, no archive) — but doesn't address the gray case of JSON-parseable non-object. The current implementation lumps all of them under corrupt-with-archive, which is defensible but means a user who saves a literal `null` (e.g. an editor's JSON-format-on-save misfire) gets an unrecoverable archive every load. Each loadState call writes a NEW archive file (line 64 unconditionally calls archiveCorruptFile), since the canonical state.json is preserved per A4. So repeated `loadState` against an unfixed state.json containing `null` produces N archive files for N calls.
impact: A persistent malformed-but-parseable state.json (rare but possible) accrues one archive file per `start` invocation. Disk usage grows linearly with start calls until the user manually rewrites or deletes state.json. No error message tells them to delete the original — only the archive paths are surfaced.
recommendation: Either (a) deduplicate archives by hash of canonical bytes (skip archiving if the most recent archive's content already matches), or (b) document this as expected behavior and rely on cycle 4's recovery flow to delete the canonical after archiving. Option (a) is more user-friendly. Lower priority than R3-001/R3-002 but worth flagging.
evidence:
// Can't determine schema_version — archive as corrupt
    const archivedTo = await archiveCorruptFile(stateDir, statePath, raw);

title: validateState failure after schema_version match archives state but contract didn't authorize it
description: When a state.json has the correct schema_version (1) but fails the shape validator (e.g. missing selected_path), loadState archives it as corrupt at line 85. Contract A4 ties the archive behavior to JSON-parse failure; A5 ties no-archive behavior to schema_version mismatch. The case 'JSON-parseable, version-matched, but shape-invalid' is contractually undefined — the implementer chose corrupt-with-archive, which is reasonable, but it means a future schema-1.x patch that adds a required field will retroactively classify all old state.json files as corrupt and archive them. This couples schema evolution to disk side-effects in a way the contract did not require.
impact: Forward-compatibility risk: any future tightening of the State shape (e.g. requiring a new ladder rung field) will, on rollout, cause every existing state.json to be archived and the canonical preserved-but-rejected. Users will see 'corrupt' warnings for files that are not corrupt — they're just from an older minor version. The contract's separation of corruption from migration intentionally avoids this conflation; the implementation reintroduces it for the validateState path.
recommendation: Treat validateState failure as a third classification 'state-shape-invalid' with no archive (mirrors schema-mismatch's no-archive policy), or bump schema_version on every shape change so this code path is unreachable. Document the choice in a comment near line 85.
